AF: Where lapis has been mined for thousands of years and every rug tells the tribe that knotted it.
AL: Silver drawn into filigree, wool woven into the qilim, walnut carved by highland hands.
DZ: Berber weavers of the Aurès and date-farmers of the oases, silver hammered by Kabyle hands.
AD: A Pyrenean co-principality of forge-iron and mountain wool, shared between two princes.
AO: Chokwe carvers of masks and thrones, coilers of sisal baskets across the highlands.
AG: Potters of Sea View Farm and weavers of the twin islands, black pineapple grown sweet.
AR: Gauchos of the pampas — leather tooled, mate gourds bound in silver, ponchos woven thick against the wind.
AM: An ancient Christian land of khachkar carvers, carpet weavers and pomegranate motifs.
AU: Merino spun soft and fine, opal split from the outback, and art from one of the world's oldest continuous living cultures.
AT: Crystal cut in Tyrol, loden woven from mountain wool, porcelain painted in Vienna.
AZ: Land of fire and carpets — pile knotted in Karabakh and silk unwound in Sheki.
BS: Androsia batik dyed by the sea and straw plaited from the silver palm.
BH: The island of natural pearls — divers' descendants who still set them in gold.
BD: The land of muslin so fine the Mughals called it woven air, and jute the world calls the golden fibre.
BB: Chalky Mount potters throwing red island clay, mahogany carved and rum aged in oak.
BY: Flax-country weavers — linen bleached in the sun and straw woven into art.
BE: Pralines boxed by hand, bobbin lace from Bruges, Flemish tapestry and Antwerp's cut diamonds — a small country with an outsized appetite for craft.
BZ: A Caribbean-Maya coast where cacao is stone-ground and hot pepper is bottled by hand.
BJ: Appliqué banner-makers of Abomey and bronze-casters of the old kingdom.
BT: The kingdom of Gross National Happiness, where the thirteen traditional arts endure — kushuthara silk woven for royalty, thangkas painted in mineral pigment.
BO: Aymara weavers of the altiplano — aguayo cloth bright against the salt and sky.
BA: Coppersmiths of Baščaršija, hammering cezves and trays under the old Ottoman roofs.
BW: Basket-coilers of the Kalahari, mokola palm dyed with bark and root, and San ostrich-eggshell beads strung by hand.
BR: Gemstones cut from the earth, golden-grass jewellery coiled in the Jalapão, berimbaus strung for capoeira — raw material turned to rhythm.
BN: A sultanate on the Borneo coast that weaves gold thread into ceremonial cloth.
BG: The valley of roses — attar distilled at dawn, pottery glazed in Troyan drip.
BF: Bronze-casters of the lost-wax method and cotton-weavers of the Sahel.
BI: Coiled-basket weavers and drummers of the great lakes.
KH: Temple-builders' descendants — silk woven ikat, silver hammered into betel boxes.
CM: Bamileke bead-throne makers and coffee-growers of the volcanic highlands.
CV: Island weavers and distillers — grogue pressed from cane, panu cloth woven fine.
CF: Wood-carvers and basket-weavers between forest and savanna — ebony worked dark, ritual masks cut by hand, raffia coiled into cloth.
TD: Leather-workers and calabash-carvers of the Sahel crossroads, silver hammered by hand where the caravan roads meet.
CL: Lapis lazuli cut blue from the Andes, copper worked in the desert north, Mapuche silver and the dark clay of Pomaire.
CN: The country that gave the world porcelain, silk and paper, still firing kilns lit before Rome was built.
CO: Coffee grown on the slopes, mochila bags woven by Wayuu hands, emeralds green as the forest.
KM: Ylang-ylang distillers of the perfume islands and vanilla-curers of the coast.
CG: Poto-Poto painters of Brazzaville and raffia-weavers of the river.
CD: Raffia-weavers of the Kuba kingdom and coffee-growers of the great forest.
CR: Painted oxcarts bright as parrots, coffee grown in the valley of the volcanoes.
HR: Lavender of the islands, the cravat the world borrowed, lace stitched three ways.
CU: Guayaberas pressed for the heat, cigars hand-rolled leaf by leaf, coffee dark as the night.
CY: Aphrodite's island — lace stitched in Lefkara, halloumi pressed by hand, silver filigree twisted fine.
CZ: Bohemian crystal cut clear as spring water, marionettes carved and strung by hand.
DK: The home of hygge — furniture curved by masters, ceramics glazed the colour of the fjord.
DJ: Salt-harvesters of Lake Assal and weavers of the Afar coast.
DM: Kalinago reed-weavers of the last Carib homeland, calabash carved by hand.
DO: Larimar the colour of the Caribbean, blue amber older than memory, cacao ground for chocolate.
EC: Panama-hat weavers of Montecristi (the hat is Ecuadorian) and rose-growers of the Andes.
EG: Land of hieroglyphs and papyrus — glass blown by hand, brass pierced into lamps, cotton spun long and fine.
SV: Añil indigo revived, La Palma folk painting bright on every surface, coffee grown on volcanoes.
GQ: Fang mask-carvers and raffia-weavers of the gulf coast.
ER: Filigree silversmiths of Asmara and coffee-roasters of the highlands.
EE: Island knitters and juniper-carvers on the edge of the Baltic.
SZ: Glass-blowers of Ngwenya and sisal-weavers of the highveld.
ET: The birthplace of coffee, where the pot, the cup and the ceremony are all part of the taste.
FJ: Masi bark-cloth beaten and stencilled, kava pounded for the ceremony.
FI: Puukko knives forged for the forest, glass shaped like Nordic ice, saunas warmed by birch.
FR: Perfume distilled in Grasse, copper beaten in Normandy — a nation that turned everyday things into luxury.
GA: Mbigou soapstone-carvers and mask-makers of the equatorial forest.
GM: Batik-dyers of the river country and drum-makers of the Atlantic shore.
GE: The cradle of wine, where cloisonné jewellery is fired and felt is rolled by hand.
DE: Solingen steel and Bavarian wood — a country that engineers even its Christmas ornaments.
GH: Kente woven strip by strip, each pattern a proverb — cloth you are meant to read as much as wear.
GR: Olive groves and island clay, a country that has been pressing, firing and weaving since antiquity.
GD: The Isle of Spice — nutmeg cracked and dried, cacao stone-ground into chocolate.
GT: Mayan backstrap looms — each village its own colour, jade carved as it was for Maya lords.
GN: Djembe-carvers of the Malinke, whose goblet drums carry the rhythms of West Africa.
GW: Cashew-growers and cloth-weavers of the Bijagós islands.
GY: Tibisiri-palm weavers and Amerindian craft-makers of the rainforest.
HT: Oil-drum sculptors of Croix-des-Bouquets and vetiver-growers whose oil scents the world's perfume.
HN: Lenca potters of the highlands and junco-palm weavers of the coast, coffee grown in cloud forest.
HK: A harbour city where tea houses and neon meet — small-batch makers in a tower of workshops.
HU: Paprika strung to dry, Herend porcelain painted petal by petal, embroidery from Kalocsa.
IS: Lopapeysa sweaters knitted against the wind, salt flaked from the North Atlantic.
IN: A subcontinent of colour — block by block, thread by thread, a different craft in every state.
ID: A thousand islands of batik, rattan and teak, each with a pattern of its own.
IR: Persia — carpets hand-knotted for centuries, saffron worth its weight in gold, enamel glowing on beaten copper.
IQ: Mesopotamia — where writing began, dates ripening in the marshes and copper beaten by hand.
IE: Aran islanders' knots and Belleek china fine as a shell — an island of makers and music.
IL: Where the Dead Sea's minerals become skincare, Jerusalem's Armenian potters glaze their blue-and-white tile, and silver is worked into Judaica and the hamsa.
IT: Glass blown on a Venetian island, leather cut in Florence — a country that made craft a way of life.
JM: Blue Mountain coffee grown in the mist, jerk spice pounded in the pestle, wood carved by roadside hands.
JP: An island that turned restraint into an art form — steel folded a thousand times, a bowl kept for the crack that makes it beautiful.
JO: Petra's country — sand bottled into art, mosaics set stone by stone, salt drawn from the sea.
KZ: Steppe nomads who felt wool into homes and hammer silver bright as the winter sky.
KE: Maasai beadwork and soapstone carved by the hillsides it comes from.
KI: Pandanus mat-weavers and shell-crafters of the low coral atolls.
KW: Dhow-builders and pearl-divers whose pantry and perfume travel the Gulf.
KG: Mountain herders whose shyrdak felt carpets carry the patterns of a hundred summers.
LA: A landlocked kingdom of looms and mountain coffee, dyed with roots and river indigo.
LV: Mitten-knitters of a hundred patterns, amber-gatherers of the Baltic shore.
LB: Cedar country — olive-oil soap, blown glass and a pantry the whole Levant borrows.
LS: Home of the Basotho blanket and the woven mokorotlo hat — a mountain kingdom that works mohair and wool against the highland cold.
LR: Mask-carvers and quilters of the Atlantic coast, country cloth woven strip by strip.
LY: Oasis date-farmers and desert silversmiths, copper beaten in the old medinas.
LI: An alpine principality famed for the stamps that collectors chase across the world.
LT: Baltic amber and linen — the sun-stone strung on thread spun from the field.
LU: A grand duchy of steel and fine tableware — Villeroy & Boch porcelain and the little Péckvillchen clay whistles sold at its spring fair.
MO: A Portuguese-Cantonese crossroads that bakes East and West into one bite.
MG: Vanilla-curers and raffia-weavers of the red island, silk woven and dyed by hand.
MW: Dedza potters and wood-carvers of the warm heart of Africa, chitenje cloth bright in every market.
MY: A crossroads of Malay, Chinese and Indian hands — pewter cast and cloth waxed by batik tjanting.
MV: Atolls where lacquer is turned on a lathe and coir is spun from the sea's coconuts.
ML: Bogolan mud-cloth dyed with river clay, kora strings plucked under the desert sky.
MT: An island of Knights — silver filigree twisted fine, glass blown in Mediterranean blues.
MR: Moorish silversmiths and leatherworkers of the Sahara, women wrapped in tie-dyed malahfa against the dunes.
MU: Model-ship builders and tea-blenders of the volcanic isle.
MX: Clay, silver and thread carrying the memory of the peoples who worked them long before the border existed.
MD: Wine-country weavers and potters — wool carpets on the loom, clay turned in the village kilns.
MC: A pocket principality of the Riviera — engraved postage stamps collectors prize, fine jewellery and confiserie on the Monte Carlo shore.
MN: Nomads of the steppe who comb fine cashmere from goats bred to weather brutal winters, felt the wool of their herds, and string the horsehair fiddle.
ME: Adriatic olive-pressers and mountain weavers between the sea and the black peaks.
MA: Where the desert meets the sea, and every souk is a workshop: leather cured in stone pits, lanterns cut by hand.
MZ: Capulana-wrappers, cashew-growers and Makonde ebony carvers of the Indian Ocean coast.
MM: Land of gold leaf and lacquer, longyi looms and jade the colour of deep water.
NA: Karakul wool-weavers and Himba craft-makers of the desert coast.
NP: In the shadow of the Himalaya, singing bowls and lokta paper made the slow way, at altitude.
NL: Delft blue fired since the Golden Age, tulips bred by the field, cheese wheeled to market.
NZ: Merino and possum blended for warmth, pounamu greenstone carved by Māori hands.
NI: Hammock-weavers of Masaya and potters of San Juan, coffee grown in the northern hills.
NE: Tuareg silversmiths of the Agadez cross, leather-workers of the caravan trade.
NG: Adire indigo tied and dyed, aso-oke woven for weddings, beads strung for kings.
MK: Ohrid pearl-makers and filigree silversmiths of the old Balkan trade towns.
NO: Fjord-country knitters and rosemåling painters, pewter cast against the cold.
OM: Land of frankincense — silver khanjars forged and rosewater distilled in the mountains.
PK: Bazaars where salt is carved into lamps and shawls are woven fine enough to pass through a ring.
PA: Guna molas stitched in reverse-appliqué, Geisha coffee grown high in the Boquete highlands.
PG: Bilum string-bag makers of the highlands and mask-carvers of the Sepik River.
PY: Ñandutí lace-makers whose 'spiderweb' cloth is spun fine as thread.
PE: High in the Andes, hands spin alpaca into knitwear and hammer silver — retablo boxes and handwoven textiles loud with colour at altitude.
PH: Islands that weave cloth from pineapple leaf and abaca, set capiz shell into windows, and terrace heirloom rice into the Cordillera.
PL: Amber some forty million years old, washed up on a Baltic beach and set by hand.
PT: Tiles that turned buildings into paintings, cork stripped by hand from trees that grow it back.
QA: A Gulf pearl-diving nation now scenting its majlis with oud and bakhoor.
RO: Carpathian weavers and potters — wool rugs, black pottery and painted monastery colour.
RU: Matryoshka nested by hand, Khokhloma flamed gold on black, Gzhel painted cobalt blue on white.
RW: Agaseke peace-basket weavers, coffee grown across a land of a thousand hills.
WS: Siapo bark-cloth painters and fine-mat ('ie tōga) weavers of the Samoan islands.
SM: One of the world's oldest surviving republics, minting collectible coins and stamps atop its mountain.
SA: The desert kingdom of dates and oud, where perfume is measured out drop by precious drop.
SN: Basket-coilers of the Sahel and sous-verre glass-painters of Dakar, boubous cut and embroidered.
RS: Opanci-shoemakers and Pirot-kilim weavers of the Balkans, preserving fruit by the jar.
SC: Granite islands strewn across the Indian Ocean — vanilla cured by hand, the giant coco-de-mer carved, pareos dyed in batik colour.
SL: Gara tie-dyers of Freetown and country-cloth weavers of the interior.
SG: A port city of Peranakan colour where kaya is jarred and tiles are hand-pressed.
SK: Tinkers who bent wire into drotárstvo art, shepherds braiding smoked korbáčik cheese by hand, the deep fujara flute carved for the hills.
SI: Beekeepers who paint their hive-fronts, salt-panners raking the Adriatic at Piran, and the lacemakers of Idrija at their bobbins.
SB: Lagoon islanders who string shell money by the fathom and carve the nguzunguzu, the spirit set at a canoe's prow.
SO: Frankincense and myrrh tapped from Boswellia in the northern hills, baskets and cloth woven along the Indian Ocean coast.
ZA: Zulu bead-workers and Ndebele painters, rooibos grown in the fynbos.
KR: A peninsula of quiet perfectionists — celadon glazed the colour of kingfisher, pantry fermented in earthen jars.
SS: Beadworkers of the Nile plains whose corsets mark age and status, and weavers of palm-leaf baskets and mats.
ES: Espadrilles woven by hand, blades forged in Toledo, azulejos glazed under the sun.
LK: An island of tea gardens and spice — cinnamon peeled by hand, gems washed from river gravel.
KN: Caribelle batik hand-dyed on the old sugar estate, coconut carved on the twin islands.
LC: Coal-pot potters of Choiseul and straw-weavers of the south, cocoa grown on the island's volcanic slopes.
VC: Basket-weavers and boat-builders of the Grenadines, arrowroot ground fine as flour.
SD: Hibiscus dried for karkade, spices ground for the pot, gold and silver worked by Nile hands.
SR: Maroon woodcarvers and Javanese batik-makers of the rainforest coast — Tembe patterns cut into wood, wax-resist cloth carried from Java.
SE: Glassblowers of the Kingdom of Crystal, whittlers of the painted Dala horse.
CH: Watchmakers of the valleys — precision in every gear, chocolate in every fold.
SY: Aleppo and Damascus — soap cured for months, wood inlaid with mother-of-pearl, brocade woven with gold thread.
TW: A mountain island that perfected oolong at altitude and turned the tea break into ceremony.
TJ: Roof-of-the-world weavers — atlas and adras silk and chakan embroidery bright against the grey mountains.
TZ: Tingatinga painters of Dar and Maasai beaders of the plains, spices grown on Zanzibar and tanzanite mined nowhere else on earth.
TH: Silk and celadon, spice and bronze — a kingdom that never stopped making beautiful things.
TL: A young nation with an ancient loom — tais cloth woven to mark every rite of passage.
TG: Ewe weavers of kente and batik, lost-wax brass cast in the southern towns.
TO: Ngatu bark-cloth beaters and pandanus mat-weavers of the Friendly Islands.
TT: Birthplace of the steelpan — oil drums hammered into music — and of Trinitario, one of the world's prized fine-flavour cocoas.
TN: Olive-pressers of the Sahel and weavers of the fouta, ceramics glazed in Nabeul blue.
TR: The bridge between two continents — hammering copper and knotting wool as Anatolia long has.
TM: Where the carpet is a national emblem — woven into the flag itself — and the knot count is a point of pride.
TV: Kolose crochet makers and pandanus-weavers of the coral atolls.
UG: Bark-cloth beaten from the fig tree, baskets coiled tight enough to hold water.
UA: Vyshyvanka stitched red on white, pysanky drawn in wax — a country that embroiders its identity.
AE: Where pearls once made the coast rich and oud still perfumes every majlis.
GB: Savile Row tailoring, Harris tweed woven in the isles — a country that dresses the world.
US: Denim riveted for miners, leather tooled on the range — a country that built its own workwear canon.
UY: Wool-throwers and mate-drinkers of the pampas, leather worked by gaucho hands.
UZ: On the old Silk Road, ikat still bleeds its colours and suzani still tells a story in stitches.
VU: Sand-drawers and wood-carvers of the volcanic archipelago.
VA: The smallest state — issuing the stamps, coins and rosaries the faithful carry home.
VE: Cacao-growers of the coast and weavers of the Andes and plains.
VN: Lacquer built up coat on patient coat, silk lanterns strung by hand, highland coffee pressed drop by drop through a phin.
YE: The mocha coast — coffee named for its port, honey drawn from the sidr tree, silver from Sana'a.
ZM: Emeralds mined green from the copperbelt, and Tonga baskets coiled tight along the Zambezi.
ZW: Shona stone-sculptors whose serpentine figures stand in galleries worldwide.
CA: Inuit soapstone carved in the Arctic, Cowichan sweaters knit on the coast, maple boiled to syrup and ammolite split from the Alberta rock.
